Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level 2 9609/23/O/N/17 Perfik Plumbing (PP) PP is a plumbing business owned and managed by Navpreet. She set up PP as a sole trader three years ago when she qualified as a plumber. The majority of Navpreetâ€™s work is repairing and fitting water pipes in customersâ€™ homes. However, there is also a growing market for fitting new appliances, such as washing machines and dishwashers, in customersâ€™ homes. Navpreet is the only female plumber in her town. This is her Unique Selling Point (USP). Her business has been reasonably successful. Navpreet has relied on recommendations from satisfied customers to promote her business. However, if PP is to grow in the future Navpreet will need to use new promotional methods. In the past, Navpreet has used a cost-based pricing strategy, but she believes she may have been setting her prices too low. question_text: Depreciation policy. In 2018 KKS plans to invest $20m in IT equipment. The current depreciation policy would be to depreciate these assets over a five year period, with an estimated residual value of $5m. The Finance Director is proposing a new depreciation policy which would reduce the period of depreciation to three years with a residual value for these assets of $8m. Marketing strategy in country X KKS used to promote its services only using trade journals and business fairs. However, three years ago KKS invested in a website and this now produces the majority of sales. This has contributed to KKS gaining a high market share in the B2B segment, supported by competitive pricing and good customer service. This segment is a low growth part of the total parcel delivery market, whilst B2C and C2C are growing rapidly. KKS is planning to integrate the marketing and distribution network IT systems. Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) software could be used to minimise the costs of moving vehicles to match customer requirements. Costs would then fall and more contracts gained. Market analysis: options A and B The Marketing Director and Finance Director have also researched the B2C and C2C segments. They have set out the following two options for expansion. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 3 9609/31/O/N/17 [Turn over Option A : Enter the B2C segment. KKS would need to make some changes to existing marketing methods. The competition is very strong, customers are price sensitive and profit margins are small. Option B : Enter the C2C segment. Competition is weak. Staff training and operational costs will be high. Parcel collection locations will be needed. A different marketing approach will be needed. The directors have carried out decision tree analysis to compare the likely returns from expanding into the B2C and C2C segments. Appendix 2 shows some results of their analysis. The directors have assumed costs and revenue will remain constant for each of the first three years. Managing the workforce KKS directors view the workforce as a key component in being the most reliable delivery service. There are 8500 employees in 64 warehouses and two administration centres. Employees are placed in one of the following departments: Operations, Administration and Finance, IT, Marketing, Human Resources. Within each department every manager has a wide span of control. KKS invests in its employees through developing their skills and enabling clear career progression. Most supervisors have been promoted from inside KKS. New employees have a training needs analysis and their line manager discusses possible training opportunities with them. Employees are encouraged to contribute to decision making. They are given encouragement to gain experience through job rotation and there is a generous bonus for good performance. In return KKS expects commitment, enthusiasm and hard work. question_text: Dividend yield 4.3% Factory Q â€“ a case for closure? ADP has expanded through external growth over the last 20 years. It has acquired some factories that perform less satisfactorily than others. Factory Q is a good example. This factory produces small domestic refrigerators. As with all ADPâ€™s factories, it is operated as a profit centre. It has recorded an adverse profit variance for the last three years. Machinery and equipment in this factory was last updated in 2005. Some directors want to close the factory and outsource production. The Finance Director has prepared a variance analysis table for Factory Q for the last three months. See Appendix 1. Market development â€“ sell in the USA? ADP does not currently sell its products in the USA. The safety standards for electrical goods are higher, and consumer protection laws stricter, than in most of ADPâ€™s markets. Consumer expectations in the USA are for high quality products. The average size of domestic appliances is bigger than the rest of the world. However, the total size of the USA market is, according to Asif, the main reason why ADP should aim to launch its products there in 2018. The Marketing Director has the responsibility for preparing a marketing plan for the sale of ADP products in the USA. He suggested to Asif that: â€˜Long term sales forecasts will be the most important factor in our new marketing plan to break into the USA market.â€™ Country Z factory â€“ improving employee performance ADP is a multinational business that takes advantage of differences in wage rates and employment laws between the countries it operates in. For example, country Z is a low income developing country. It has few employment laws to protect workers. ADP benefits from this situation and pays its employees in its country Z factory less than 50% of the average ADP wage rate. This ADP policy of treating employees in different countries in different ways was defended by the Human Resources Director at a press conference. She held this press conference after an accident in the country Z factory that badly injured four employees. â€˜It is true that this factory does not have the safety equipment that we install in our other factories around the world. However, ADP meets all its health and safety obligations under the law in country Z. Our employees here are paid more than other similar workers in the country. Unemployment is high so they know that other opportunities are limited.â€™ The Operations manager of ADPâ€™s factory in country Z is aware that employee performance is a real problem for the company. Based on full costing Â• Variable feed costs per litre = 8 cents Â• Dairy farm labour costs per litre = 6 cents [9000/150 000] 1 Â• Overhead costs allocated to farm = 0.05 x Â£15.6m/52 = $15 000 per week = 10 cents per litre 2 Â• Total full cost per litre = 24 cents 3 Â• Cost of buying in milk = 20 cents Â• Total cost saving per week = 150 000 x $0.04 = $6000 4 Â• Annual cost saving [additional profit] = $6000 x 52 = $312 000 5 Marginal/contribution costing Â• Cost of buying milk 20 cents. Â• Variable cost of dairy farm = 0.08 + 0.06 = 0.14 2 Â• Thus, extra contribution of dairy relative to buying milk is 0.06 per lit 4 Â• So contribution of milk division is really $9000 per week 5 Â• So profit would fall by $468 000 per year if the farm/milk division was closed. 6 6

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Short Answer and Essay May/June 2018 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/M/J/18 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜market researchâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages to a business of primary (field) market research. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜market researchâ€™. The process of collecting data about customers or markets or competition (1) and analysing such data (1). Finding out what consumers want or need (1) before a product is made (1). Process of gathering primary or secondary data (1) using research methods (e.g. surveys, interviews and product testing) (1) Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two advantages to a business of primary (field) market research data. The advantages of primary (field) market research data could include: Primary market research is first-hand data directly related to the needs of a business. Â• It is up-to-date and thus more useful than dated secondary research. Â• Relevant â€“ it is collected for a specific purpose and answers the questions a business asks. Â• The business has direct control over what and how information is collected. Â• The information collected is confidential and can be hidden from competitors. Â• Allows the targeting of customer groups. Sound explanation of two advantages of primary research data. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or partial explanation of two advantages. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 5 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜productivityâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways a manufacturing business could improve productivity. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜productivityâ€™. The effectiveness/efficiency of productive effort (1) the amount of output produced (1) against the inputs used to produce (1) a measure of efficiency (1) the contribution of a worker (1) in a certain time (1). Productivity could be expressed as a formula for 2 marks: Output Productivity Time Period (2) OR Output No. of employees (2) Sound definition â€“ 2 of the one-mark factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two ways a manufacturing business could improve productivity. Answers may include: Â• Improve the training of employees to raise skill levels. Â• Improve employee motivation â€“ intrinsic and extrinsic rewards. Â• Invest in more advanced technology. Â• More efficient management of employees. Â• Set clear goals and measure performance. Â• Recruit better employees. Â• Give better feedback. Â• Improve quality assurance/control. Â• Reduce waste. Â• Introduce Kaizen/cell production. Â• Improved communication. Sound explanation of two ways to improve productivity. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain how ethics may influence the objectives of a business. Answers could include the following: Â• The moral principles that guide the way a business behaves â€“ distinguishing between â€˜rightâ€™ and â€˜wrongâ€™ and then making the â€˜rightâ€™ choice. Â• The objectives of a business may include survival, growth, market domination, profit, and the pursuit of any of these may well involve ethical dilemmas. Ethics may influence business objectives in the following ways: Â• Ethics prevent a business pursuing negative objectives such as profit maximisation at any cost. Â• Prevents a business exploiting workers to maximise profits by the government setting minimum wage levels. Â• Prevents a business exploiting suppliers to maximise profits (preventing late or slow invoice payments). Â• Prevents a business deciding on a low cost location in a country that has few regulations e.g. child labour, lax pollution laws, poor health and safety, bribery, slavery. Â• Encourages CSR. Â• Encourages recycling. Â• Encourages employee welfare. Â• Encourages sustainable production. Effective explanation of how ethics may influence the objectives of a business. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of how ethics may influence the objectives of a business. (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of ethics/objectives. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 7 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜margin of safetyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two limitations of break-even analysis. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜margin of safetyâ€™. The amount by which the sales level exceeds the break-even level of output (2) OR an indication of how much sales could fall without the business falling into loss (2) OR the difference between the actual output and the break-even level of output (2) OR actual/budgeted sales â€“ break-even sales (2). The amount by which a business has passed its break-even level (1) Sound definition is for any one of the 2â€“mark definitions shown above (2 marks) Partial definition is for the 1â€“mark shown above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain two limitations of break-even analysis. Answers could include the following: Â• It is supply side only (costs). Â• It tells you nothing about what sales are actually likely to be for the product at various prices. Â• Not all costs can be conveniently classified into fixed and variable costs. Â• It assumes that fixed costs are constant/they can increase Â• Variable costs do not always stay the same. Â• Sales are unlikely to be the same as predicted output. Â• Break-even analysis assumes all output is sold. Â• There is likely to be some build-up of stock and/or wasted output. Â• Assumes external environment is stable. Â• Depends on accuracy of data â€“ it is only a forecast. Â• It is only useful for one product. Sound explanation of two limitations of break-even analysis. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one limitation of break-even analysis or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one limitation or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 8 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse why accurate cost data is important for all businesses. [8] (b) Discuss why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit. [12] 6 â€˜A business mission statement is only important if it directly affects the strategy and tactics of that business.â€™ Discuss this view. [20]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse why accurate cost data is important for all businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of why accurate cost data is important for all businesses 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of why accurate cost data is important for all businesses 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of why accurate cost data is important for all businesses 3â€“4 1 Understanding of cost data 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Accurate cost data is at the heart of current and future operations and decisions of business organisations. Â• If costs are not recorded accurately, then managers have incomplete /misleading information on which to make decisions. Â• Setting prices (e.g. cost-plus) requires accurate cost data. Â• Calculations of gross margin/gross profit will lead to misleading information in the Income Statement if cost data is not accurate. Â• The Balance Sheet will not be accurate unless inventory costs are correct. Â• The valuation of assets on the Balance Sheet will be inaccurate if inventory costs are incorrect. Â• Cost analysis is vital if costs are to be controlled and resources used efficiently. Â• Cash flow forecasts need to be accurate if they are to be useful. Â• Break-even estimates rely on accurate costing. Â• With accurate cost data a business can run more effectively â€“ e.g. sales prices can be set to a more competitive margin. Â• Helps with good decision-making â€“ e.g. investment of funds in new directions/cost cutting for savings both depend heavily on correct cost estimates. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of why a business should focus on managing its cash and/or making a profit 3â€“6 1 Understanding of new business/cash/profit 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Cash flow is critical for new businesses. Â• It is common for profitable businesses to run out of cash. Â• It is vital to have sufficient cash in the short term â€“ profit may have to wait for the long term. Â• It is important that the distinction between cash and profit is recognised by the business. Â• New businesses are often given much less time to pay suppliers, given shorter credit periods. Â• Banks and lenders will require quick and agreed time repayment. Â• Finance may be difficult for new businesses to obtain, so they need to build up their own cash reserves. Â• So, cash flow management is critical for new business survival. Â• The process of generating revenue requires cash, so especially for a new business, cash is more important than profit. Â• A focus on cash in the early days of a new busin

mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜inventory managementâ€™. The activities involved in maintaining an appropriate/optimal amount/ level of inventory (1) to assist cash flow and keep costs low (1). To ensure uninterrupted production/service levels (1). The management/control/organisation of stock items/suppliers (1). Overseeing ordering of inventory / storage of inventory (1). The efficient control of stock levels (1). One type is JIT (allow other methods) (1). 1(b) Briefly explain two reasons why a business might decide to hold a high level of inventory. The reasons for holding a high level of inventory could include: Â• A buffer against anticipated high demand. Â• Respond to seasonal variations in demand. Â• To eliminate/reduce the risk of stock-out costs. Â• In anticipation of future supply problems. Â• To provide sufficient levels of inventory to meet customer demand. Â• To take advantage of discounts for bulk purchasing. Â• To respond to anticipated future price rises by suppliers. Â• To improve customer service with high levels of product/service availability. Â• The stock is not perishable/does not deteriorate. Â• To reduce transportation costs. Â• There is extra space available. Answers could relate to a specific business or a specific product within a business. Sound explanation of two reasons for holding a high level of inventory. Â• Market conditions and operational opportunities provide a range of pricing options for the business. Â• The price elasticity of demand for a product (e.g. inelastic demand) may provide an opportunity for a business to adopt a price discrimination strategy. Â• The train company can sell different priced tickets and maximise revenue and potentially increase profits and profitability. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Short Answer and Essay May/June 2018 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/13/M/J/18 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜leadershipâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜leadershipâ€™. Leadership involves giving clear direction / vision for a business (1), setting an example and engaging others to follow (1), motivating / inspiring / guiding a group of people (1) towards achieving a common objective / goal / aim (1). Giving long term direction to an organisation / taking the business forward (1). Giving a new direction for a business (1). Definitions only of â€˜managementâ€™ such as setting objectives, organising resources, controlling, etc., that mention none of the above leadership definitional features should be awarded 0 marks. Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. Explanations could include: Â• Autocratic / authoritarian â€“ all decisions kept at top of the organisation or with the leader â€“ impact on employees / business operations. Â• Democratic â€“ workers encouraged to take part in decisions â€“impact on employees / business operations. Â• Paternalistic â€“ leader will listen and consult but will take decisions themselves as â€˜they know bestâ€™ â€“ impact on employees / business operations. Â• Laissez-faire â€“ â€˜hands offâ€™ approach â€“ opposite of autocratic â€“ most decisions left to workforce â€“ impact on employees / business operations. A â€˜soundâ€™ explanation of a style of business leadership (2 marks) should comment on the impact of that style on employees or the business. A â€˜partialâ€™ explanation will simply define a style of leadership with no reference to impact. Sound explanation of two styles of business leadership. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one style or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one style or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 5 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜operations managementâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways in which changes in technology might affect the operations management of a business. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜operations managementâ€™. The process of managing resources e.g. labour / capital / stock at the operational level (1) to achieve efficient production (1) and provision of goods / services (1). Managing/planning the transformation process â€“ inputs into outputs (1) managing the production process (1). Deciding what to produce (1). Deciding on method of production e.g. using technology (1). The scale of production (1). What materials to use (1). The process of adding value (1). Quality control (1). 2(b) Briefly describe two ways in which changes in technology might affect the operations management of a business. Answers could include the following: Â• Technological change / advancement could mean greater efficiency in productive process. Â• However, failures in newly installed technology may cause disruption to production. Â• Might require business to purchase new technically advanced machinery â€“ new investment required. Â• Changes in technology will take time to implement. Â• Businesses that do not invest in and use new technology may fall behind competitors. Â• Impact on employees â€“ new training required to work new advanced machinery. Â• Might mean fewer staff in operations management. Sound explanation of two ways operations management might be affected. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why the objectives of a business may change over time. Answers could include the following: Â• Business objectives are the stated measurable targets (tactical, strategic, corporate, departmental) that move an organisation to achieve its aims and purpose. Â• Objectives can include survival, growth, profit maximisation, sales growth, socially responsible aspirations. Â• Changes may take place in response to: â€“ â€“ Initial objectives achieved (survival). â€“ Competitive environment changes. â€“ New leadership and management. â€“ Technology changes â€“ new production possibilities. â€“ New opportunities arise â€“ international trade. â€“ Economic / external situation changes â€“ e.g. recession. â€“ Unique products are no longer unique. â€“ Customer demands change. â€“ Change of ownership. An effective explanation of why the objectives of a business may change over time will clearly identify specific objectives that may change and will give an explanation of at least two. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why the objective(s) of a business may change over time. (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of business objectives. (1 mark) No creditable content. 4(b) Briefly explain, with examples, two other types of business costs. Answers could include the following costs: Â• Fixed costs â€“ costs that do not vary with output in the short-term e.g. rent of premises. Â• Variable costs â€“ costs that change with output e.g. cost of materials used in making a computer. Â• Semi-variable costs â€“ these have a fixed and variable element, e.g. the basic wage of a salesperson (fixed) and commission earned (variable). Â• Direct costs â€“ costs that can clearly be identified with each unit of production and can be allocated to a cost centre, e.g. the wages of a garage mechanic. Â• Indirect costs â€“ costs that cannot be clearly identified with a unit of production and cannot be allocated directly to a cost centre â€“ e.g. the cost of cleaning a retail shop. Sound explanation of two other types of business costs, with an example of each. question_text: Analyse the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business. [8] (b) Discuss the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business 5â€“6 2 Some explanation / application of the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business 3â€“4 1 Understanding of venture capital / source of finance / small business 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Venture capitalists are organisations or wealthy individuals who lend risk capital for start-up or expansion. Â• Small companies may find it difficult to raise capital even if they are profitable. Â• Venture capitalists supply essential financial backing for small businesses. Â• They are usually easy to locate and many available at a relatively low cost. Â• They are usually under strict supervision by regulatory bodies. Â• Angel investors operate on a smaller scale. Â• There is no repayment schedule â€“ it is an investment so there is no repayment of debt as a cost of doing business. Â• An immediate source of temporary finance unavailable elsewhere. Â• Venture capitalists can also provide important advice, guidance, and consultation â€“ can help with financial management. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of the importance of non- financial and/or financial measures of business performance 3â€“6 1 Understanding of non-financial / financial measures of business performance 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Focus on quantitative financial data such as financial ratios seen as limiting and possibly misleading. Â• Financial data can be historic and manipulated. Â• Financial data gives only a partial picture of performance and misses out measures such as innovation, quality, employee relations, intangible assets â€“ the qualitative drivers of performance. Â• Non-financial factors such as customer loyalty, brand value, intellectual capital may well point to a significant part of the value of a business. Â• Financial factors take no account of such factors. Â• Non-financial measures may well be more closely linked to the long- term performance of a business while traditional financial measures may place too much emphasis on short term issues â€“ focus on strategy rather than just tactics. 7(a) Analyse the potential advantages to a community of a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the potential advantages to a community of a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the potential advantages to a community of a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets 5â€“6 2 Some explanation / application of the potential advantages to a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets 3â€“4 1 Understanding of community / triple bottom line / business targets 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: The â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ approach goes beyond the traditional measures of profits, return on investment, and shareholder value to include social and environmental dimensions â€“ profits, people and the planet. A triple bottom line business may provide benefits to a community such as: Â• Economic â€“ make profits to reinvest back into the business and the local / national economy as well as to shareholders. Â• Social â€“ provide jobs or local support to disadvantaged sections of the community. Â• Environmental â€“ protect the environment and manage the business in an environmentally sustainable way. Â• These 3 benefits may be more valuable to a community than the traditional targets of a private sector profit driven business. The triple bottom line organisation (social enterprise) has the capacity to temper the extremes of a purely capitalist / profit-centred approach to business activity and offer much more socially responsible and valuable business performance. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 12 of 12 Question Answer Marks

The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/22/M/J/18 Umpire Umbrellas (UU) UU is a private limited company that sells umbrellas with pictures it prints on them. All of the pictures on the umbrellas are printed using batch production. The umbrellas can be used to shelter from the rain or as a shade from the sun. The company has two main markets: consumer and business. UU sells individual umbrellas to consumers. These have pictures of famous people or places on them. They are mainly sold through UUâ€™s website. UU produces 20 000 of each design. The business market for UU is made up of businesses that want their logo printed on a large number of umbrellas. UU guarantees all business orders are delivered within 10 days. Recently, UU received an order from a large bank for 10 000 umbrellas with the bank logo printed on them. The bank gave an umbrella to any customer who opened a new account. The fixed cost for the order was $2000 and the variable costs were $0.75 per unit. UU made $3000 profit on the order. UUâ€™s Board of Directors has been considering two different options to grow the business. Option 1 : UU would sell franchise opportunities to entrepreneurs who can print and sell UU branded umbrellas to the consumer market in the entrepreneurâ€™s local area. These entrepreneurs would pay a fee to UU as well as 10% royalties on the profits. In return UU would provide the machinery needed to print the umbrellas. The franchisee would be required to purchase all of their inventory from UU. Option 2 : UU would increase its product portfolio by selling other goods to the business market, such as pens, USB sticks and clothing. This would require external finance to purchase the machinery needed to be able to print on these products. The market for these is growing but is very competitive. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜consumerâ€™ (line 4). [2] (ii) Briefly explain the term â€˜variable costsâ€™ (line 11). [3] (b) (i) Calculate the total revenue of the order from the large bank (lines 7â€“12). [3] (ii) Explain one reason why UU needs accurate cost data. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Insert. DC (LK) 168733/1 [Turn over Cambridge Assessment International Education Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 7 7 5 7 5 0 7 8 2 7 * BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay May/June 2019 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/M/J/19 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜competitive pricing strategyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain why a business might use a price skimming strategy. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜competitive pricing strategyâ€™. A pricing strategy / method of pricing where a price for a product or service is set / made (1) Based upon the price set by competitors (1). A business offers to match the price of competitors (1). Set a price slightly below competitors (1). An example Mcdonalds v Burger King (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain why a business might use a price skimming strategy. Answers could include: Â• Price skimming is the setting of a high price for a product that is considered to be unique or having special features and thus has a low price elasticity of demand (inelastic). Â• May be a new product â€“ aim is to maximise short-term profits before competitors enter the market. Â• Create customer perception of high quality. Â• Customers can equate quality with high price. Â• Producers of luxury goods/designer goods focus on status conscious customers who will pay high prices. Â• Recoup high costs involved in production (e.g. pharmaceutical companies). Â• To maximise profit. Â• To exploit a product with a low elasticity of demand. Sound explanation of the advantages of a business using a price skimming strategy. (3 marks) Partial / limited explanation of the advantages of a business using a price skimming strategy. (2 marks) Explanation / reference to a price skimming strategy. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜retained earningsâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain the distinction between short-term and long-term sources of business finance. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜retained earningsâ€™. The profits made by a business that are not distributed to shareholders (1). Profits left after all deductions have been made / dividends have been paid (1). Profits re-invested in the business (1). Profits kept as a reserve (1). A source of internal finance (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain the distinction between short-term and long-term sources of business finance. Answers could include: Short-term financing . Â• Usually refers to financing that spans a period of less than a year but could extend according to the types of loan/debt being considered. Â• Loan requiring a short repayment period, probably at a lower interest. Â• Risk is lower so more accessible to smaller businesses. Â• Include short-term loans, overdrafts, short-term leases, accounts payable. Â• Used for day to day financial requirements / to cover any shortfall between income and expenditure. Long-term financing Â• Spans a longer period of time, from over 1 year â€“ 30 years. Â• Long-term financing is riskier, so banks often require collateral to prevent default on repayment. Â• Includes share issue, bonds, long-term bank loans, long-term leases, retained earnings, mortgages. Â• Restricted to large businesses, established businesses that can supply collateral. Â• Used for longer term/larger investment projects. Answers may make reference to features of short-term and long-term sources of business finance such as those listed above. Accurate references to these features are acceptable as explanations of the distinction between these two sources of business finance. Sound explanation of the features of short-term and long-term sources of business finance. (3 marks) Partial / limited of the features of short-term and long-term sources of business finance. (2 marks) Explanation / reference to sources of business finance. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 6 of 14 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain factors that could influence the scale of operations of a business. Answers could include: Â• The size of the operations of a business from small / limited to large / extensive. Â• The objectives of the owners of a business â€“ there may be reluctance to expand a small business. Â• Capital available may be limited â€“ growth will be restrained. Â• The size of the market that a business operates in may be relatively small â€“ or a deliberate choice for a niche market. Â• The extent of competition â€“ intensive competition may limit scale of operations. Â• Possibility / scope for economies of scale â€“ may offer opportunities for expansion. Â• State of the external economy may affect scale. Â• Legal restrictions may affect opportunities to expand. Â• Size of labour resource available. Â• Nature of product â€“ less standardised products such as handicraft

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜private limited companyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two possible disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜private limited companyâ€™. A business owned by private shareholders (1). A small to medium-sized company (1). Owned by shareholders (1). Shareholders have limited liability (1). Shareholders are often members of the same family (1). The business is a separate legal entity (1). Cannot sell shares to general public / stock exchange (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain two possible disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. Answers could include: Â• Loss of 100% ownership of the business. Â• Loss of control of the business (might not be a majority shareholder). Â• Cost of changing to a private limited company. Â• May lose influence over key decisions such as distribution of profit, future growth policies. Â• Needs to produce end of year accounts â€“ more transparency of financial affairs. Â• Information disclosure could make a business competitively disadvantaged. Sound explanation of two disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one disadvantage or partial explanation of two disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one disadvantage or a list of two disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 8 of 14 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. [8] (b) â€˜A democratic style of leadership is the most effective leadership style for a manufacturing business in a very competitive industry.â€™ Discuss this view. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of autocratic/laissez-faire leadership. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Â• An autocratic leader has complete command over employees. Â• Leader takes all major decisions. Â• Views / ideas / suggestions are discouraged. Â• Criticism not allowed. Â• Can lead to speedy decision making and high productivity. Â• Can lead to poor employee motivation and high labour turnover. Â• Works best with unskilled, monotonous, routine work where leader is best performer. Â• A laissez-faire leader is one who trusts employees / team to perform the job themselves. Â• Armâ€™s length management. Â• Views are shared and suggestions welcomed. Â• Works best when employees are highly skilled, loyal, and experienced. Â• Works best with employees who are subject area specialists â€“ capable of accepting responsibility. Â• Should not be used to mask managerial incompetence. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 9 of 14 Question Answer Marks

5(b) â€˜A democratic style of leadership is the most effective leadership style for a manufacturing business in a very competitive industryâ€™. Discuss this view. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the view that a democratic style of leadership is the most effective leadership style for a manufacturing business in a very competitive industry. 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the view that a democratic style of leadership is the most effective leadership style for a manufacturing business in a very competitive industry. 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of the view that a democratic style of leadership is effective in a business. 3â€“6 1 Understanding of democratic style of leadership / manufacturing business / competitive industry. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: Â• An initial definition of democratic leadership style might be given â€“ key features. Â• References to the competitive business environment may include a discussion of the requirement for a business to be a brand leader, or innovative/different, or flexible, or efficient and effective â€“ to compete. Â• Such a competitive position may need to be built on a motivated workforce â€“ sound operational management decisions â€“ perhaps stimulated by a democratic distinctive senior management leadership. Â• However, could lead to slow decision making and business falls behind the competition. Â• Could lead to more mistakes being made. Evaluation could include: Â• Strong candidates may well refer to the manufacturing business context and question the importance of democratic leadership with possibly unskilled/routine work and where capita

7(a) Analyse the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using Just in Time (JIT) to manage its inventory. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using JIT to manage its inventory. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using JIT to manage its inventory. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using JIT to manage its inventory. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of JIT / retail business. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Â• Definition of JIT â€“ avoid holding high levels of inventory to require supplies to arrive just as they are needed. Â• Advantages and disadvantages of JIT set out and analysed in the context of retail â€“ great variety of products â€“ need to consistently meet customer demand â€“ limited space for holding inventory â€“ products may be fragile, perishable. Â• Advantages â€“ capital investment in stock reduced as is opportunity cost of stockholding â€“ cost of storage reduced â€“ more space to display products â€“ danger of out-of-date stock reduced â€“ reduced risk of wastage â€“ quicker response to customer demand. Â• Disadvantages â€“ failure of supply delivery could mean empty shelves, product shortage â€“ delivery costs (frequent) may be high â€“ may not be able to benefit from bulk buying (depends on the size of the retail business) â€“ very dependent on quality of supplier (it may, of course, be its own delivery service in a large retail business). It is often difficult for retailers to accurately calculate customer demand. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

Â• Flexibility might be achieved by having a flexible and adaptable workforce, flexible flow-line production equipment, increase capacity by purchasing more equipment / logistics / supply chain management. Â• Innovation is the use of new or improved processes e.g. production / design / R&D / work practices / systems. Â• Innovation might be achieved by developing new products or new production processes / CAD and CAM / training and restructuring of the workforce. 1(a) Define the term â€˜supplyâ€™. The quantity of a product / service that businesses are prepared to supply / produce (1). 1(b) Briefly explain two non-price factors which could increase the demand for a product or service. Answers could include: Â• Increase in consumer income. Â• There is limited supply. Sound explanation of two non-price factors which could increase the demand for a product or service. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜business leadershipâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜business leadershipâ€™. Motivating / inspiring staff (1). To achieve objectives / goals / aims (1). Directing an organisation (1). Having a vision for a business / organisation (1). Having a strategy for a business (1). Â• Definitions that simply refer to management functions such as control, coordination, resource allocation should not be awarded marks. Â• If, however, such an answer includes the activity motivation then this should be regarded as a partial answer and be awarded 1 mark. Â• Listing types of leadership should not be awarded marks. Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. Autocratic / authoritarian: all decisions taken at the top of the organisation, or by the leader. Democratic: employees encouraged to participate in decisions. Laissez-faire: â€˜ hands offâ€™ approach, opposite of autocratic, most decisions left to employees. Paternalistic: leader may listen and consult but will take decisions as â€˜they know bestâ€™. Â• A sound answer may be one that (a) defines a style of leadership as above and then (b) briefly explains some aspect of that style such as impact on employees, impact on quality of decisions, speed of decisions, resulting empowerment, increased delegation. Such an answer should be awarded 2 marks (for each style). Â• A partial answer is one that perhaps gives a single definitional sentence with no additional explanation. Such an answer should be awarded 1 mark (for each style). Sound explanation of two styles of business leadership. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one style of business leadership or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one style of business leadership or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. Â• Different stakeholders in a typical business â€“ employees â€“ management â€“ shareholders â€“ customers â€“ suppliers â€“ local economy â€“ local / national government â€“ pressure groups. Â• Business decisions of interest to stakeholders include: profit distribution â€“ expansion â€“ diversification â€“ supplier policy â€“ degree of social responsibility â€“ operating methods â€“ remuneration and motivating policies. Â• Different stakeholders will have views on such business decisions that may lead to conflict among the stakeholders or between some stakeholders, e.g. shareholders vs. employees with regard to profit distribution â€“ so decisions could be changed or overruled or supported with or without conflict. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain why a business might choose flow production as a method of operation. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. Production of a one-off / unique product or service (1) specially designed by / for a customer (1). One product made at a time (1). Labour intensive production process (1). Requiring a highly skilled work force (1). A method of production (1). A relevant example such as a piece of jewellery (1). Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain why a business might choose flow production as a method of operation. Answers could include: Â• Producing items in a continually moving process might be the best production for the business, e.g. in production of identical standardised cars on an assembly line. Â• Can make use of high technology methods â€“ robot arms. Â• Can mass produce items to a consistent standard. Â• Offers economies of scale and low costs for a one-size-fits-all product â€“ possible price reductions and increased sales can result. Â• Can produce different content products on the same production line (e.g. Coke, Sprite). Â• The market demands high volume, capital is available, unskilled workforce available might suggest a choice of flow production. Sound explanation of the advantages of flow production which cause a business to choose this method of operation. (3 marks) Partial / limited explanation of the advantages of flow production which cause a business to choose this method of operation. (2 marks) Explanation / reference to flow production. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 8 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. [8] (b) â€˜Financial measures are the most important indicator of business performance.â€™ Discuss this view. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. 3â€“4 1 Limited understanding of crowd funding / source of finance / small businesses. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Â• Small businesses typically have significant problems in raising funds. Â• The practice of funding a project / venture by raising monetary contributions from a large number of people, typically via the internet. Â• Access to cheap(er) money for small and medium sized businesses. Â• May not have to pay interest or be repaid. (However, in some instances crowd funding may have interest and repayment.) Â• No need to sell an equity stake in the business. Â• Fast and flexible source of finance. Â• Allows many more speculative projects to be funded. Â• Supports business expansion. Â• Acts as a marketing / brand tool. Â• May give feedback and advice to project fund seeker. Â• Entrepreneurs do not have to risk own money for a new venture. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

5(b) â€˜Financial measures are the most important indicators of business performance.â€™ Discuss this view. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the view that financial measures are the most important indicators of business performance. 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the view that financial measures are the most important indicators of business performance. 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of the view that financial or non-financial measures are important indicators of business performance. 3â€“6 1 Understanding of financial / non-financial measures / business performance. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: Â• In recent years, there have been moves towards supplementing financial measures of performance such as financial results data and ratio analysis. Nevertheless, quantitative measures of business performance are important and strong answers may well outline the value of financial measures before discussing the case for including the qualitative performance measures. Â• Financial measures could include the following: profit, cash flow, break even, liquidity (current ratio, acid test ratio), profitability (gross profit margin, net profit margin and return on capital employed). Reference may also be made to income statements and statements of financial position. Â• Recognition of the limitations of financial data and often too little emphasis on the qualitative drivers of performance, such as innovation, quality, employee relations, intangible assets. 2(b) Briefly explain two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. Answers could include: Â• Concern to produce output at the highest ratio of output to input. Â• The business might be capital or labour intensive or a combination of both â€“ will offer different opportunities. Â• Improve / increase employee training / recruitment â€“ more skills. Â• Improve staff motivation / morale. Â• Job rotation to reduce monotony. Â• Invest in more advanced equipment â€“ e.g. robotics. Â• Change the actual system of production â€“ e.g. to flow production. Â• Decrease waste. Â• More efficient management / supervision. Â• Better communication channels. Â• More appropriate inventory management. Sound explanation of two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. Answers could include: Â• Important decisions are made in a business such as: â€“ what suppliers to use â€“ what price to set â€“ how much to invest â€“ when to expand etc. Â• Such decisions cannot realistically be made without accurate cost information. Â• Cost information informs the business decision-making process. Â• Cost information facilitates control / monitoring / benchmark comparisons. Â• All resource allocation decisions are dependent on cost information. Â• Vital for setting budgets and targets. Â• Cost information is at the heart of all important business decisions. Â• Credit examples of specific decisions. Â• Compare costs with competitor costs. Â• Know when the break-even point is reached. question_text: Data Response May/June 2019 1 hour 30 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/23/M/J/19 Fuzz Buzz Ltd (FBL) FBL is a small private limited company with 10 shareholders. Its unique selling point is that its toys are handmade. They are aimed at children aged 4 to 10. Its latest product, a hand-held soft spinning toy, called Tommy Turner, has become a fashion item amongst young children. Demand for these toys exceeds the ability of FBL to supply them. Stores are desperate to secure more inventory in order to make the most of the high demand. FBL sells these toys for $6 each. It is considering how to respond to the excess demand. If FBL increases the price of the Tommy Turner then the price elasticity of demand is estimated to be â€“2. The quantity demanded at the current price is 120 000. If FBL increases the supply of Tommy Turners it would require investment. The new machinery would cost $100 000 and allow mass customisation. The concern is that this fashion may not last. The work will be a lot less skilled, although a design team will still be required to develop future toys. FBLâ€™s employees take great pride in their work and are very loyal to the company and their co-workers. Most of the employees have been with the company for over 10 years and enjoy good relationships with all the directors. Anindita, the Marketing Director, thinks FBL should make the most of the fashion for the Tommy Turner by extending the product life cycle. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜unique selling pointâ€™ (line 1). [2] (ii) Explain the term â€˜mass customisationâ€™ (line 10). [3] (b) (i) Calculate the new quantity demanded of the Tommy Turner if FBL increases the price by 10%. [4] (ii) Explain one factor influencing the supply of the Tommy Turner. [2] (c) Analyse two methods FBL could use to extend the product life cycle of the Tommy Turner. [8] (d) Discuss the likely impact on the morale and welfare of FBLâ€™s employees if supply of the Tommy Turner is increased. [11] 5 10 15

INFORMATION â— The total mark for this paper is 40. â— The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. 2 9609/11/M/J/20 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. Define the term â€˜leadershipâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two qualities of a good leader. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜leadershipâ€™ . The art of motivating/inspiring/guiding a group of people. (1) To achieve a common or shared goal. (1) Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Explain two qualities of a good leader . Answers could include: â€¢ Self-confidence and/or self-belief. Giving them belief that they can make good decisions. â€¢ Intelligence. The ability to judge and be sure of actions to be taken. Able to determine the future or best direction for a business. â€¢ Charisma. Enabling them to convince people to follow their lead. Charismatic leaders are often followed without question. They can inspire others to achieve the goals of the business. â€¢ Creativity. The ability to be innovative so that the business will stand out from its rivals. Sound explanation of two qualities of an effective leader (3 marks) Sound explanation of one quality or partial explanation of two qualities (2 marks) Limited explanation of one quality or a list of two (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜opportunity costâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two reasons why many new businesses fail in their first year of operation. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜opportunity costâ€™. The value of the next best opportunity. (1) That is given up by taking a particular decision. (1) Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two reasons why many new businesses fail in their first year of operation. Answers could include: â€¢ The product or service being offered is not wanted by consumers. â€¢ A lack of business/management experience causes poor decisions to be made. â€¢ A lack of finance. A new business might have to finance its operations for some time before income is sufficient to cover costs. â€¢ Lack of market research or poor market research resulting in flawed information meaning that the expected demand does not happen. â€¢ A sudden change in the economy that causes consumers to change their spending patterns. â€¢ A reaction from established competitors â€“ perhaps competitors increase their advertising or reduce the prices of their products, making it more difficult for the new business to survive. Sound explanation of two reasons why many new businesses fail in the first year (3 marks) Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two reasons (2 marks) Limited explanation of one reasons or a list of two (1 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 6 of 14 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why it is important for a manufacturing business to manage its inventory effectively. Answers could include: â€¢ A definition of inventory/inventory management. â€¢ A need to hold sufficient raw materials or components to meet the needs of the manufacturing process. This might depend on whether or not the business uses JIT. â€¢ Failure to hold sufficient inventory could lead to the production process being halted until new stock arrives. This can cause resources to be idle i.e. labour and machines. â€¢ An inability to manufacture could result in a failure to meet customer delivery requirements. This could cause a loss of future orders for the business. â€¢ Too much inventory is also an added cost to any business e.g. storage costs, insurance, etc. Effective explanation of the importance of effective inventory management to a manufacturing business (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the importance of effective inventory management (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of inventory management (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜price discriminationâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two disadvantages to a business of using price discrimination. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜price discriminationâ€™. When a business charges a different price. (1) In different markets or to different consumers. (1) For the same product. (1) Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (1 marks) No creditable content (0 marks) 2 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 7 of 14 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Explain two disadvantages to a business of using price discrimination. Answers could include: â€¢ If any two sets of customers have contact, there is a possibility that they will discover that they are being charged different prices and therefore their custom might be lost. â€¢ It might result in a loss of trust in the business if customers feel that they have been misled. â€¢ The administration might be complex in order to ensure that the price charged is the price agreed. â€¢ There is a possible loss of profit if the business agrees to sell to one set of customers cheaper than to others. Sound explanation of two disadvantages to a business of using price discrimination (3 marks) Sound explanation of one disadvantage or a partial explanation of two disadvantages (2 marks) Limited explanation of one disadvantage or a list of two (1 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 8 of 14 Question Answer Marks

Benefits of an increase in market share include: â€¢ An increase in the number of customers â€“ increased sales. â€¢ The business becomes more well-known with a possible increase in brand/image recognition leading to stronger image. â€¢ Increased scale of operation leading to economies of scale. â€¢ Lower unit costs leading to increased profit or the opportunity to reduce price to consumers and therefore become more price competitive. â€¢ If the business gains a large market share the business is likely to be more powerful in the market and might be able to dictate prices. Brand leaders can set prices. â€¢ Retailers might be very eager to stock that brand of goods â€“ able to gain a more prominent sales position in retail outlets. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 9 of 14 Question Answer Marks

7(a) Analyse the benefits to a new business of using break-even analysis. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the benefits to a new business using break-even analysis 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the benefits to a new business of using break-even analysis 5â€“6 2 Some application of the benefits of using break- even analysis 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of break-even analysis 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Possible answers might include: â€¢ Break-even analysis is a method used to find the level of output where the total revenue from a product is equal to the total costs of producing/providing that product. Break-even is where TR = TC. â€¢ New businesses often struggle to achieve a profitable situation in the short term; break-even analysis can allow the new business to calculate the level of output that would be necessary to achieve a given level of profit. â€¢ The business might be uncertain of what price can be charged for their product/service; break-even analysis will enable them to test different price options. â€¢ It can also indicate if costs are too high and might indicate that cheaper options should be explored. â€¢ Break-even analysis is a relatively simple technique that can be completed by more inexperienced owners/managers. â€¢ A new business might be inexperienced at managing costs and using break-even analysis can provide guidelines for the business to work to. â€¢ The desired level of profit, the level of output and the price are dependent on consumer demand; completing a break-even analysis is a forecast of these levels, but not a guarantee. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

If the liquidity ratios are good then the business should not experience any problems with paying suppliers etc. and therefore should not cease to trade due to a cashflow problem. â€¢ Profitability ratios measure different types of profit relative to sales revenue. A business selling expensive jewellery is likely to have a high gross profit margin and a high value added. â€¢ A business selling expensive jewellery would be unlikely to expect a quick turnover of inventory. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜private sectorâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two advantages that a business in the public sector may have that a business in the private sector may not. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜private sectorâ€™. Businesses owned and controlled by an individual or groups of individuals . (2) Not owned/controlled by the government. (1) Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two advantages that a business in the public sector may have that a business in the private sector may not. Answers could include: â€¢ Possibly easier access to finance because they are funded by a government. â€¢ They are not under pressure to make a profit because they are often/usually providing essential goods and/or services. â€¢ They do not have to pay dividends to shareholders. â€¢ They are frequently large businesses that benefit from economies of scale. Sound explanation of two advantages for a business in the public sector. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or a partial explanation of two advantages (2 marks) Understanding of private/public sector businesses. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 6 of 13 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain the difficulties that a small business start-up might have when trying to obtain finance from an external source. Possible answers might include: â€¢ The business will not have any past financial history to present to potential lenders. â€¢ Banks might be unwilling to allow a bank loan/overdraft to an inexperienced business owner. This might depend on the previous experience of the person/people starting the business. â€¢ Small businesses might find it difficult to arrange external finance because of their lack of collateral/security. They are unlikely to have many assets for security. â€¢ Difficulty of persuading lenders that the business if viable if the product/service is very new to a market. Effective explanation of the difficulties that a small business start-up might have in obtaining an external source of finance. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the difficulties that a small business start-up might have in obtaining an external source of finance. (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of the difficulties of business start-up/external finance. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) Two points well developed can be rewarded in the 4â€“5 bracket. 5 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Explain two advantages to a business of selling in international markets. Answers could include: â€¢ An international market means that a business will target consumers at home and overseas which might give access to more customers. â€¢ Can allow economies of scale to be gained allowing costs per item/service to be reduced leading to more profit. â€¢ Larger scale activity than a local/national market might allow a business to offer variations to meet the needs of different consumers thus spreading risk. â€¢ Overseas markets might result in new ideas for the business. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the disadvantages to a business of using debt factoring to improve its cash flow. [8] (b) Discuss the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. [12] 6 â€˜Good leaders are more important for business success than good managers.â€™ Discuss the extent to which you agree with this view. [20]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the disadvantages to a business of using debt factoring to improve its cash flow. Answers could include: Debt factoring is when a business sells its debts to a third party. Disadvantages: â€¢ The full value of the debt is not paid by the debt factor (usually about 80% of the value of the debt is received). â€¢ Debt factors usually refuse to take the long-term bad debts so the business still has debts that it might struggle to recover. â€¢ Customers might not like having their debt sold to a third party because they will now have to deal with someone outside of the original business they dealt with. â€¢ Customers might not want to deal with the business again. â€¢ Use of debt factoring could send a signal to stakeholders that the business has liquidity problems. This could impact on the relationship of the business with its stakeholders. For example, if it is a public limited company, shareholders may begin to sell shares believing that the business is no longer a good investment. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 8 of 13 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the limitations of comparing accounting ratios between businesses. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of accounting ratios. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Possible answers might include: This can depend on why the ratios are being compared and by whom â€“ if it is a potential investor then they will need a lot more information. Comparisons can only be reasonably made between business in the same industry and of the same size. E.g. a large business is likely to have more purchasing power than a small one and is likely to receive discounts which reduces costs. This will be reflected in ratio analysis. Ratios can show trends over time and between businesses but should be used with caution because: â€¢ The accounting techniques used differ between businesses â€“ different methods of depreciation can affect the profitability ratios making it difficult to make like-for-like comparisons. â€¢ Ratios are calculated using past information. The present situations in different businesses might be quite different. One business might be researching and introducing a new product shortly which could alter its ratios significantly now and in the future. â€¢ Ratios are not a certain indication of what is happening now or in the future. â€¢ Ratios do not give any qualitative information such as the level of motivation of the employees. This can impact on future performance and therefore ratios. â€¢ The values used from the statement of financial position are taken on one specific day. The results of the ratios

Answers could include: â€¢ The high cost of preparing all the documentation required to become a public limited company / to float the company on the stock exchange. â€¢ Shareholders will no longer have to be approved by existing shareholders; any member of the public can purchase shares in the business. â€¢ Becoming a public limited company can give the business a higher profile meaning that any problems in the business might be more in the public eye. â€¢ Divorce of ownership and control. question_text: UFilters (UF) UF is a public limited company selling to an industrial market. It manufactures air conditioning units. UF produces two sizes of air conditioning units, medium and large. Most of its air conditioning units are sold for use in warehouses and computer server rooms. The units are expensive compared to competitors but UF believes its units are of a higher quality. Market research suggests that there is a growing demand for small air conditioning units to be placed in offices. To produce a new size of unit would require spending $180 000 to purchase new machinery. UF relies on customer recommendations for new orders. However, some customers have been dissatisfied with the service received. UF has received several complaints, including: â€¢ engineers turning up late or not at all â€¢ poor communication from UF â€¢ little choice in the size of units supplied â€¢ appointment times are not always convenient and can be difficult to change. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: The Shop (TS) Thomas worked for 30 years as a manager of a factory. Although he was very good at his job he was recently made redundant. Thomas always wanted to open a shop. He thinks he has the qualities an entrepreneur is likely to need for success. He has undertaken some primary market research to identify possible opportunities in city X where he lives (see Fig. 1.1). bakery shop 20% clothes shop 5% sandwich shop 25% electronics shop 10% butcher shop 10% coffee shop 15% grocery shop 15% Question: What type of shop do you think would be most successful on Main Street? Percentage of respondents for each type of shop, based on a sample of 160 shoppers on Main Street in city X between 12:00 and 14:00 on Friday
mark_scheme_text:
1(a)(i) Define the term â€˜redundantâ€™ (line 2). Knowledge Marks A correct definition 2 A partial, vague or unfocused definition 1 No creditable content 0 Content: Redundancy is where an employee is made to leave an organisation because their job role is no longer required. 2

1(a)(ii) Explain the term â€˜primary market researchâ€™ (line 4). Award one mark for each point of explanation: Knowledge Marks A Example or some other way of showing good understanding 1 B A n understanding of primary 1 C A n understanding of market research 1 Content: â€¢ Market research is when a business investigates the needs of consumers to assess who may be interested in purchasing its product/service. â€¢ Primary involves the business conducting the research themselves, first- hand. â€¢ Examples include questionnaires, surveys etc. â€¢ Can be more specific than secondary, more expensive than secondary etc. ARA 3 9609/22 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 4 of 11 Question Answer Marks

Answer = 24 Correct figures 160 shoppers in total Coffee shop = 15% Formula 160 X 15 / 100 = 24 respondents Rationale Marks Correct answer with or without correct working 2 Correct identification of figures 1 No creditable content 0 2

1(b)(ii) Explain two limitations of the sampling used by Thomas. question_text: Super Heroes (SH) SH is a leisure (theme) park aimed at 10â€“18 year olds. It is owned by two companies, X and Y, which started SH as a joint venture. Company X owns many leisure centres and swimming pools. Company Y owns many brands based on superheroes. SH employs 200 full-time workers and an extra 50 seasonal workers during the busiest times of the year. The park has 10 large rides which take up 2 km 2 of land. There are also many smaller rides, restaurants, toilets and shops. The price of an entrance ticket is $11 per customer. Table 1.1 shows the costs for SH in 2019. 4(b) Analysis â€¢ Planning of when resources are needed and their use can save time and money thus increasing profit â€“ Fixtures and fittings can be ordered to arrive just in time for their installation in week 18 so cash outflows can be delayed. â€“ Targets set will help motivate staff and increase efficiency Evaluation â€¢ Network presented is relatively simplistic â€“ more detail needed â€¢ Doesnâ€™t guarantee that project will be completed on time â€¢ HBG has experience of planning building work so should be relatively accurate 9609/32 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 13 of 17 Question Answer Marks 5 Recommend the changes that HBG should make to its marketing strategy to achieve its objectives of increasing membership and vis
