question_text: Define the term â€˜market segmentationâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two benefits to a business of market segmentation. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜market segmentationâ€™. â€¢ an approach to marketing (1) â€¢ identifies different sub-groups within a market (1) â€¢ targeting different products to each market segment / type of customer (1) â€¢ segmentation might be due to age / social factors / income levels / geographic / sociological factors. (1) â€¢ customers with similar needs/characteristics (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 7 of 18 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain two benefits to a business of market segmentation. Answers could include: â€¢ better able to target potential customers. â€¢ target only segments that have high demand to maximise profits. â€¢ marketing costs can be lowered due to more specific targeting. â€¢ marketing personalised to maintain brand loyalty. â€¢ different strategies can be used for different segments. â€¢ products/services can be designed/developed to satisfy very specific needs of a group of consumers. â€¢ businesses can possibly use price discrimination by charging different prices to different sub-groups/segments. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two benefits to a business of market segmentation. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one benefit to a business of market segmentation or a partial explanation of two benefits. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one benefit to a business of market segmentation or a list of two benefits. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 8 of 18 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain the advantages to a manufacturing business of using sale and leaseback of non-current assets as a source of finance. Answers could include: â€¢ Where a business sells an asset in order to raise finance and leases the same asset back from the new owner. â€¢ The business will have an inflow of money which could be large if the non-current asset is, for example, a building. This can be used to fund projects or equipment. â€¢ The business will still have access to and use of the asset after the sale, therefore the sale should not cause any disruption to the operation of the business. â€¢ The payment of regular lease payments can allow for ease of budgeting for a business. â€¢ The maintenance of the asset is usually taken over by the new owner, therefore reducing ongoing maintenance costs for the business when it leases the asset back. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of the advantages to a manufacturing business of using sale and leaseback of non-current assets as a source of finance. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the advantages to a manufacturing business of using sale and leaseback of non-current assets as a source of finance. (2â€“3 marks) Explanation of sale and leaseback. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 Question Answer

question_text: Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two advantages to a business of changing from job production to batch production. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. â€¢ production of one-off items, one-by-one (1) â€¢ specialised/unique items designed by/for customers (1) â€¢ hand-made/limited supply (1) â€¢ each item finished before next one produced (1) â€¢ correct example e.g. designer dresses, watches (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two advantages to a business of changing from job production to batch production. Answers could include: In batch production, groups of identical products are produced together. â€¢ economies of scale/ cost reduction as the scale of production increases. â€¢ speed of production will be increased due to use of machinery and less skilled workers due to repetition of tasks. â€¢ less capital because the same equipment can be used for multiple products unlike the often very specialised equipment used in job production. â€¢ customer demands can still be met but in the production of a batch of several products rather than one individual product. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two advantages to a business of changing from job production to batch production. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage to a business of changing from job production to batch production or a partial explanation of two advantages. (2 marks) Limited explanation of one advantage to a business of changing from job production to batch production or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 9 of 19 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain the importance of working capital to a new business. Answers could include: â€¢ working capital is used to finance the day to day running of a business. â€¢ A new business has to cover its current liabilities with its current assets if it is to remain viable. â€¢ it can take some time for a new business to become known and to attract sufficient customers to generate profit. Working capital is essential to help the business survive these early days. â€¢ a new business had to finance its start-up and then needs a flow of money e.g. to be able to pay for labour and inventory. â€¢ if the premises are rented/mortgaged then regular payments will have to be made. â€¢ resources are likely to be purchased and so money needs to be available to pay for this. â€¢ suppliers are less likely to offer credit terms so all supplies will have to be paid cash on delivery. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of the importance of working capital to a new business. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the importance of working capital to a business. (2â€“3 marks) Explanation of working capital. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 10 of 19 Question Answer Marks

5(a) â€¢ An increase in market share might require the business to reassess the range or products or services being offered. Perhaps a competitor offers a wider range or a better quality. â€¢ An increase in market share often needs a business to stand out and therefore the business might need to look at whether or not its products are outdated and perhaps there is a need to introduce extension strategies or to introduce new product(s). â€¢ Linking to the product life cycle, if the business has too many products in the maturity or decline stages this would indicate that new products are required if the marketing objectives are to be achieved. â€¢ New markets might require products to be adjusted/changed to meet the needs of the customers in that market. â€¢ Successful businesses often have products in all stages of the product life cycle; new products being developed to take the place of ones in decline or to meet the changing needs of the market. â€¢ The range of products offered will have to be adjusted to meet the marketing objectives and if the objectives change the products might have to change too. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 13 of 19 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss the extent to which a new entrant in the computer manufacturing market will need to rely on penetration pricing to gain market share. mark_scheme_text:
7(a) Analyse the benefits to a business of accurate cost information. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the benefits to a business of accurate cost information. 7â€“8 3 Limited analysis of the benefits to a business of accurate cost information. 5â€“6 2 Application of the benefits of cost information. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of cost information. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and Understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of cost information Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to the benefits of cost information Analysis 4 marks â€¢ Managers need cost information to be able to assess and monitor the expenses of the business. â€¢ Without cost information the price of products/services might be set too low and costs would not be covered by the selling price. â€¢ The level of costs determines the level of profit. If the cost information is inaccurate the business might be expecting to make a profit that does not happen: the business might even make a loss. â€¢ Inefficiencies can be highlighted by looking at the cost information for different parts of a business and actions can be taken to correct any problems. If the information is not available or is inaccurate the inefficiency might go unnoticed. â€¢ Inaccurate cost data might incorrectly indicate an efficiency or inefficiency and the wrong action might be taken. â€¢ Managers can compare trends over time and monitor any changes and take corrective action. â€¢ The important element is that cost information needs to be accurate. Having lots of cost data that is not accurate will be of little use to managers and might lead them to make decisions that are damaging to the business. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 18 of 19 Question Answer Marks

7(b) Discuss the usefulness of liquidity ratios to potential investors in a business. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the usefulness of liquidity ratios to potential investors in a business. 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the usefulness of liquidity ratios to a business. 7â€“8 2 Analysis and application of the usefulness of liquidity ratios to a business. 3â€“6 1 Knowledge and understanding of liquidity ratios / investors 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and Understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of liquidity ratios â€¢ Clear understanding of investors Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to liquidity ratios â€¢ Reference to the role of business investors Analysis 2 marks â€¢ Liquidity ratios measure the ability of a business to pay its current liabilities. â€¢ Two key definitions: The acid test ratio and the current ratio. Acid test = current assets inventory current liabilities âˆ’ Current ratio = current assets current liabilities â€¢ For most businesses, the current ratio is an adequate measure of liquidity but for a business that holds a high level of inventory then a safer measure might be the acid test

1(b) Explain two responsibilities of a stakeholder group. Answers could include: â€¢ employees: to work according to their contract of employment; to co-operate with management; be honest and diligent; obey codes of conduct. â€¢ managers: to make decisions in the best interests of the business; honesty, ethics, social responsibility. â€¢ employees and managers: protect company information from competitors. â€¢ customers: to be honest in their dealings with the business e.g. not steal; not to exploit a business by unfair claims; pay for goods on time. â€¢ suppliers: supply goods/materials of the desired quality; deliver on time; charge a reasonable price. â€¢ lenders of finance: to supply finance on the terms agreed; to allow repayment in the time agreed. â€¢ government: intervene on behalf of other stakeholders; pollution, consumer protection, employee interests; support via grants/subsidies. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜economies of scaleâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two possible causes of diseconomies of scale. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜economies of scaleâ€™. â€¢ reduction in costs of production/operations. (1) â€¢ due to increase in size/scale of production / output. (1) â€¢ correct diagram showing economies of scale. (2) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two possible causes of diseconomies of scale. Answers could include: â€¢ Communication diseconomies: increased output leading to an increase in the workforce, making it more difficult to communicate directly with employees. Less direct methods of communication might need to be used which might be less effective. Feedback from employees/departments is likely to be more difficult to obtain. â€¢ Co-ordination diseconomies: more people, more departments and possibly more functions within a business make the monitoring and managing of individual performances or processes more difficult to achieve, making co-ordination of activities more difficult. Timing of activities â€“ it might become more difficult to ensure that activities are completed at the same/required time. The values of the business might not be employed to the same extent in each section of the business e.g. ethical values, environmental values. â€¢ Control diseconomies: employees can feel more alienated in a larger organisation and the quality standard might fall. More output can make it more difficult to check each item for quality. It is more difficult to control several departments on numerous sites than to monitor and control the functions within a smaller business. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two possible causes of diseconomies of scale. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one possible cause of diseconomies of scale or partial explanation of two causes. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one possible cause of diseconomies of scale or list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 9 of 19 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Explain why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a small business. [5]
mark_scheme_text: 3(a) Explain why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a small business. Answers could include: â€¢ Cost-based pricing is determined by the costs of production of a product/service. â€¢ Cost plus pricing - the full costs of a product are calculated and a margin is added to achieve the desired profit level. â€¢ Contribution cost pricing â€“ in a small business, it is likely that the fixed costs per product are known due to the small scale of the business, making it easier to include a proportion of them in the price of each item produced. â€¢ A small business needs to ensure that all the costs of production are covered by the price charged to its customers. â€¢ The fixed costs together with all variable costs will be included and a percentage added to give a profit. â€¢ This can ensure that the business recovers all costs when goods are sold. â€¢ The amount of profit added on to the costs of the business can be adjusted to make sure that the business is able to compete on price with its competitors. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a small business. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a business. (2â€“3 marks) Explanation of cost-based pricing. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 5 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 10 of 19 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜labour turnoverâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two ways in which the recruitment and selection process of a business could help to improve labour turnover. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜labour turnoverâ€™. â€¢ The rate at which employees leave a business (2) â€¢ Correct formula Number of employees leaving (in a year) 100 Number of employees in the business (in a year) Ã— (2) â€¢ Employees leaving the business. (1) â€¢ In a given time period. (1) â€¢ Through dismissal, resignation, redundancy, new employment. (1) Sound definition will be either the first bullet point or the formula or 2 of the other 3 bullet points. (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of bullet points 3, 4 or 5. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Explain two ways in which the recruitment and selection process of a business could help to improve labour turnover. Answers could include: â€¢ be sure that the job description accurately describes the tasks and responsibilities of the job to recruit the most suitable people. â€¢ be sure that the person specification is focused on the qualities and qualifications required when recruiting staff. â€¢ Investigate/analyse previous employeesâ€™ reasons for leaving. â€¢ use an appropriate medium for advertising any posts e.g. social media, recruitment sites or agencies, local newspapers, depending on the role. â€¢ check that applicants have the relevant essential and desirable qualities. If not, then reject the application. â€¢ use an appropriate selection technique or a combination of methods e.g. an aptitude test followed by an interview. It is possible that using only one technique might not give the most reliable results. For example, some people are able to interview well but might not perform as well in an aptitude test. â€¢ the more thorough/appropriate the recruitment and selection process is, the more likely it is that the best candidate is selected. If the person is right for the job, it is less likely that they will leave, therefore labour turnover will improve. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two ways. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two ways. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or list of two ways. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 11 of 19 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch production to flow production. [8] (b) Discuss the benefits to a clothing business of holding buffer inventory rather than using a Just in Time (JIT) inventory system. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch production to flow production. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch to flow production. 7â€“8 3 Limited analysis of the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch to flow production. 5â€“6 2 Application of the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch to flow production. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of batch/ flow production. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and Understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of batch production. â€¢ Clear understanding of flow production. Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to change from batch to flow production. Analysis 4 marks â€¢ Batch: the production of a limited number of identical products. Each stage of production is completed before the next stage commences. â€¢ Flow: the production of identical products in a continuous flow. Disadvantages can include: â€¢ The high cost of setting up a flow production method. More equipment might be involved that adds to the costs of production in the short-term. â€¢ Fewer employees might be needed, causing some redundancies, leading to demotivated workforce. â€¢ High levels of output usually required to make flow production a profitable option. Is demand of the business high enough to justify the change? â€¢ If there is a fall in demand it is more difficult to switch to the production of different products because flow production usually has dedicated equipment for the production on one product. â€¢ If the business produces a wide variety of products, batch production might be more efficient. Non-productive time would occur while the flow production equipment was being switched to the production of a different product. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 12 of 19 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Hot Bubblez (HB) Karen is a sole trader who operates a service business called Hot Bubblez (HB) from her own home. It is a laundry service in town P. Each day Karen spends the morning collecting laundry from customersâ€™ homes. Karen then washes, dries and irons the laundry and then delivers it back to customersâ€™ homes in the evening. She owns two washing machines, two drying machines and one iron. Each batch of washing takes approximately two hours to wash, dry and iron. Karen has completed some market research about which method of laundry households use in town P (see Fig. 1.1). There are 60 000 households in town P. Use a laundry service 20% Do laundry at home 65% Go to a laundry shop (launderette) 15% Fig. 1.1: The percentage of households using each laundry method in town P Karen has 300 households that use HBâ€™s laundry service. Karen would like to employ someone to do the collections and deliveries. Karen has always relied upon customer recommendations to promote HB. However, she has not been able to gain any new customers and she thinks it is time for new promotion methods to be used. She would like to target the hotel laundry market. Table 1.1 shows some data about the local hotel market. Table 1.1: Data about the local hotel market Number of hotels in town P

The term â€˜marketâ€™ has a number of different meanings. Any legitimate definition can be used. The most common definition (as a means of trade) is likely to include: â€¢ A system/place/facility whereby products are bought (1) and sold (1) â€¢ Where businesses and consumers (1) trade (1) However other definitions may include: Market as a group of potential customers for a product: â€¢ The customers/consumers (1) who may potentially purchase a product (1) â€¢ The businesses (1) who are competing to sell a product (1) â€¢ The group of people (1) who the business is trying to sell to (1) Market as a verb, i.e. to market a product â€¢ The combination/mix of decisions (1) that a business uses to target a group of customers (1) Exemplar Marks Rationale Where buyers and sellers come to trade. 2 A correct definition. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Bilalâ€™s Homes (BH) Bilal is a sole trader, who buys and sells apartments for a profit. Bilal left university two years ago and moved back to his parentâ€™s home in city W. He lacks social skills but he has always been good at self-management, so he wanted to start his own business. Bilal bought his first apartment using money that he borrowed from his parents. He spent some time and money improving and upgrading the apartment and was able to sell it for a profit. Bilal paid his parents back and then had enough capital to purchase his second apartment. Since then, Bilal has bought three more apartments. Table 1.1 shows some financial details about the last three apartments that Bilal bought and sold. Table 1.1: Financial details about the last three apartments that Bilal bought and sold Apartment Purchase price ($) Cost of improvements and upgrades ($) Selling price ($) A 58 000 8 000 80 000 B 120 000 10 000 150 000 C 65 000 15 000 70 000 Bilal has done some market research and has found out that customer demand for buying apartments in city W is increasing. He knows that he needs to purchase as many apartments as he can before the prices increase. However, he is limited by the capital he has in the business. Bilal has been approached by Sophie, another entrepreneur, who also buys and sells apartments. Sophie would like to enter into a business partnership with Bilal. She has provided Bilal with some details from the last year about her business. Bilal can use this data to help him decide if he would like to enter into the partnership (see Table 1.2). Table 1.2: Details about Sophie and her business in 2020 Number of apartments bought and sold

You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. Define the term â€˜social enterpriseâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜social enterpriseâ€™ . A business with mainly social objectives (1) such as environmental or social projects (1) A business which reinvests profits into the business (1) A business that reinvests profits to benefit society/community (1) rather than maximise returns to owners (1) A business that focuses on triple bottom line or corporate social responsibility (1) Often described as not-for-profit businesses/organisations (1) such as charities (1) Clear definition of 2 of the factors listed above 2 marks Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2

1(b) Explain two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise . Answers could include: ï‚· A social enterprise might attract more customers just because of its aim of benefitting the community ï‚· The business might be seen as more ethical than other businesses which again can increase demand for its product/service ï‚· People might be willing to pay a higher price because the business regards people and the planet as more important than profit ï‚· Employees might be easier to recruit as people seek to work in social enterprises ï‚· Employees might be easier to retain leading to lower labour turnover ï‚· Employees are committed and motivated by the values and aspirations of a social enterprise ï‚· Government support/grants/subsidies might be available unlike in businesses with purely a profit motive Accept any other valid response Clear explanation of two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise 3 marks Clear explanation of one advantage or a partial explanation of two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise 2 marks Partial explanation of one advantage to a business of being a social enterprise or a list of two advantages 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 8 of 18 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜emotional intelligenceâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two benefits to a business of autocratic leadership. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜emotional intelligenceâ€™. The extent to which managers are aware of and understand their own emotions and feelings (1) and the feelings and emotions of others (1) Reference to Golemanâ€™s 4 competencies (1) Clear definition of 2 of the factors listed above 2 marks Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2

2(b) Explain two benefits to a business of autocratic leadership . Answers could include: ï‚· Employees know clearly that they are expected to follow orders ï‚· There is close supervision of employees so fewer mistakes ï‚· Quicker decision-making. Less time spent/wasted consulting employees before decisions are made ï‚· Productivity may improve due to no time wasted on discussion ï‚· Less conflict due to no discussion ï‚· Clear communication channel from the owner/leader (clear who gives instructions and who follows them) ï‚· Non-participating employees are more focused ï‚· More experienced/qualified/skilled leaders take decisions Accept any other valid response . Clear explanation of two benefits to a business of autocratic leadership. 3 marks Clear explanation of one benefit to a business of autocratic leadership or a partial explanation of two benefits of autocratic leadership. 2 marks Partial explanation of one benefit to a business of autocratic leadership or a list of two benefits 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 9 of 18 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain how price elasticity of demand influences the pricing decisions of a business . Answers could include: Price elasticity of demand measures the response of demand to any change in the price of a product. ï‚· Behaviour will depend on whether the PED of products is elastic or inelastic ï‚· Price elastic â€“ a business wishing to increase market share can reduce price knowing that demand for its products will increase by a greater percentage than the percentage decrease in price. This achieves an increase in total revenue ï‚· Price inelastic â€“ if an increase in profit is a business aim, a business might decide to increase the price of its products knowing that the percentage fall in demand will be lower than the percentage increase in price = increase in total revenue. ï‚· If a business wants to increase market share, a decrease in price would possibly not be as effective as for a price elastic product â€“ the percentage increase in demand for price elastic products would be smaller than the percentage decrease in price = reduced revenue and only a small increase in demand ï‚· PED will influence the establishment by a business of an ideal price for a product in the marketing mix ï‚· The way in which PED might influence businesses can depend on the objectives of the business ï‚· A business that wishes to maximise its revenue might behave differently to a business that wishes to maximise sales to gain market share Accept any other valid response. Items that will be in use for a long period (more than 1 year) which managers must plan for ï‚· Revenue expenditure is money used to pay for everyday running costs of a business, e.g., raw materials or components, tea or coffee for the canteen or paper for the offices. These items will be used in the short term and, even though lower value, must still be planned for ï‚· They should normally be funded differently; working capital / retained capital ï‚· Revenue expenditure recorded correctly allows a manager to accurately measure the cost of the day-to-day running of a business which is vital information in the early days of a business. ï‚· They are recorded on different financial documents: revenue expenditure is recorded in the Income Statement; capital expenditure is recorded in the Statement of Financial Position â€“ managers use these documents to assess the financial situation of a business Accept any other valid response. question_text: Define the term â€˜price elasticity of demandâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two benefits to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜price elasticity of demandâ€™ . Correct answers must refer to the responsiveness of the demand for a product. Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the degree of responsiveness of demand (1) to a change in the price of the product/service. (1) % change in quantity of demand for a product PED = % change in price of the product (2) Clear definition of 2 of the factors listed above or formula 2 marks Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 11 of 20 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Explain two benefits to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products. Answers could include: ï‚· Allows a business to anticipate the reaction of its customers to a change in price and to forecast the level of sales following a change in price ï‚· Pricing strategies (e.g. price discrimination) are influenced by an understanding of how demand might be affected by price changes ï‚· Maximises revenue, i.e. price inelastic â€“ revenue rises when price of product increases; price elastic â€“ revenue rises when price of product falls ï‚· Helps a business to decide on its pricing policy, e.g., a product with a PED greater than 1; a change in the price of the product would result in a percentage change in the level of demand which is higher than the percentage change in price. This might lead a business towards competitive pricing ï‚· A business can use the knowledge that demand for their product is inelastic to know that an increase in the price would lose proportionately fewer customers than the price increase. Businesses that provide goods that customers view as essential know that an increase in price will not harm demand significantly Accept any other valid response. Clear explanation of two benefits to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products 3 marks Clear explanation of one benefit to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products or a partial explanation of two benefits 2 marks Partial explanation of one benefit or a list of two benefits 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 12 of 20 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜corporate social responsibilityâ€™ (CSR). [2] (b) Explain two disadvantages to a business of having CSR as a business objective. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜corporate social responsibilityâ€™ (CSR). Corporate social responsibility is when a business considers the impact of its activity (1) on all/different stakeholders. (1) Business policies to show the commitment of a business to the well-being of society (1) and the environment. (1) Goes beyond legal responsibilities (1) Clear definition of 2 of the factors listed above 2 marks Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2

2(b) Explain two disadvantages to a business of having CSR as a business objective. Answers could include: ï‚· CSR is likely to involve additional costs for a business ï‚· A business that fails to meet its CSR aims/claims (e.g. window-dressing in published accounts) might suffer a loss of reputation and credibility ï‚· Social responsibility might mean different things in different countries/cultures so can make it difficult for businesses that trade regionally or internationally. It can be hard for a business to understand what is most important in terms of CSR in different countries/regions ï‚· Stakeholder conflict: different stakeholder groups have different expectations of any business and might want a business to focus on different issues during its activities ï‚· Too much focus on CSR and not enough on profit Accept any other valid response. Inventory is the amount of goods held ready for sale or components ready for use in a business. Answers could include: ï‚· Items can go out of fashion resulting in inventory being unsold or unused but there will still have been a cost to the business ï‚· Storage space might be limited therefore limiting the amount that can be held ï‚· Storage conditions would need to be suitable to prevent deterioration of inventory. Some items held in stock can become damaged and then cannot be sold or used at all ï‚· Items might be made/obtained to order when required (JIT) rather than being held in stock ï‚· New businesses might only be able to afford to hold low levels of inventory ï‚· A business might be involved in specialised work for which there is not a high demand Accept any other valid response. mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a partnership. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a partnership 7â€“8 3 Limited analysis of the disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a partnership 5â€“6 2 Application of the disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a partnership 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of sole trader or partnership 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and understanding 2 marks ï‚· Demonstrates a clear understanding of being a sole trader ï‚· Demonstrates a clear understanding of being a partnership. Application 2 marks ï‚· Reference to the disadvantages of changing from a sole trader to a partnership business. Analysis 4 marks ï‚· Loss of ownership. The sole trader might have been the sole owner of their business for some time and this change would be difficult to take, knowing that they no longer fully own their business. ï‚· Shared decision-making. New partners might not agree with the approach of the original owner. Previously all decision-making was done by the sole owner of the business. Agreement would need to be reached with the new partner(s) which would take time. Any advantage of quick decision-making might be lost ï‚· Shared profit. Previously any profit made was for the sole owner but after taking in partners any profit would have to be shared between them ï‚· Responsible for the actions of the partner(s). The actions of one partner are binding on all other partners. If one partnerâ€™s actions result in a large debt, then all partners are liable for that debt whether they agreed with the action or not. Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 12 of 18 Question Answer Marks

Content: A co-operative is a business structure where the workers are the owners. The profits are shared with workers. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Great Resources (GR) GR is a business partnership that creates educational resources. It sells direct to schools and teachers via its own website. Sanjay, Rukmal and Boris are entrepreneurial teachers who formed the GR partnership. One year ago, they identified a gap in the market to supply interactive, digital resources. GRâ€™s website is subscription only. An online marketing campaign, which used penetration pricing, attracted 250 subscribers in the first six months of operation. The start-up costs were financed with a $5000 bank overdraft, which is GRâ€™s only debt. Reviews for GRâ€™s products in teaching journals are positive but cash flow is poor. Many customers have taken advantage of a recent sales promotion for one monthâ€™s free membership and posted positive reviews. Unfortunately, few have then taken out a regular subscription. As revenue has not increased as much as the entrepreneurs had hoped, they must now consider alternative promotion methods. They have researched possible promotion methods and decided to advertise in an educational newspaper. The newspaper has a readership of half a million people. Expert Materials (EM) is a large national company that also advertises in the newspaper. EM is GRâ€™s closest competitor. The EM brand is well-known and trusted in the educational resources market. Table 1.1 shows some marketing data. Table 1. question_text: Office Furniture Designs (OFD) Markus is an entrepreneur and is planning to set up a private limited company called OFD in four weeksâ€™ time. OFD will be an online retailer selling office furniture directly to business customers. Products will be sent directly to customers from OFDâ€™s warehouse. OFDâ€™s product range will include office chairs, desks and filing cabinets. Markus will have limited start-up funds. OFD will operate in a competitive market and Markus is keen to offer high-quality customer service. He also will have to think carefully about opportunity cost. As part of his business planning, Markus has prepared a cash flow forecast (see Table 1.1). Table 1. This is a new and expanding market in country J, targeting the urban youth market. MXBâ€™s business objectives are reviewed regularly by Rani, the Managing Director. Rani is concerned that MXBâ€™s sales of mountain bikes have declined by 12% over the past year. Demand for mountain bikes in country J has remained static. 1(a)(ii) Exemplar and annotations Mark Rationale The next best alternative that has been given up . For example, if Markus chooses to spend money on customer services and not marketing then the opportunity cost would have been the loss of the marketing benefits . 3 An answer which starts with the knowledge and then applies it to Markus. The next best thing forgone when making a decision . A business could choose to buy some desks , but they would miss out on being able to buy chairs . 3 Again, knowledge first, followed by application to a decision (choose to buy some desks) and to the lost benefit (not being able to buy chairs). If a business has Â£100 and they choose to buy inventory instead of fuel , then the opportunity cost is the lost benefit of the fuel because it has been lost . 3 A different way of answering the question. There is application of both elements of opportunity cost (the choice and the lost benefit) as well as clear knowledge of the concept by the end of the response. The next best opportunity forgone . For example, when you choose to buy a chocolate bar . 2 Clear knowledge. Some application of a choice, but not the lost benefit. What you miss out on when a decision is made . 1 Not a perfect definition, but enough to award the knowledge mark. No attempt to apply. Indicative content AO1 Knowledge and understanding. Just-in-time production minimises the time, labour, and materials in a manufacturing process . question_text: Analyse two limitations of using the number of employees to measure the size of a business. [8] (b) Evaluate whether small retail businesses have an important role in the economy. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two limitations of using the number of employees to measure the size of a business. There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two limitations of sampling for a business: 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one limitation of using the number of employees to measure the size of a business ï‚· there are many ways to measure the size of a business and most are more reliable than using the number of employees ï‚· business that relies on machines rather than people will be undervaluedâ€“fails to take account of capital investment ï‚· does not take account of the assets of a business ï‚· does not take account of the cash flow of a business ï‚· no reference to revenue, output, sales, profit ï‚· workers can be full time or part time ï‚· workers can be outsourced ï‚· workers can be multi-skilled so fewer needed 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 19 of 29 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of external growth. question_text: Analyse two limitations to a business of using contribution costing. [8] (b) Evaluate whether working capital is the most important source of finance for a start-up furniture manufacturer. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two limitations to a business of using contribution costing. There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two limitations to a business of using contribution costing: 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one limitation to a business of using contribution costing ï‚· It allocates only direct costs to cost/profit centres ï‚· Overhead costs not apportioned at all ï‚· May provide a misleading picture of profitability ï‚· It overlooks the impact of fixed costs ï‚· Not acceptable for external financial reporting ï‚· Difficult to allocate costs accurately across a full product range ï‚· Not an appropriate method for price determination ï‚· Unsuitable for capital intensive operations/multi-product operations 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 19 of 30 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for application of one limitation to a business of using contribution costing ï‚· Difficult to analyse as not all costs can be easily split into fixed and variable costs. ï‚· Poor method for setting prices â€“ as prices are based on contribution which does not cover fixed costs. ï‚· It violates the principle of matching costs with revenues ï‚· Has limited scope â€“ so businesses with high automation have high levels of fixed costs, so not suitable to capital intensive industries. ï‚· Can lead to unrealistic financial statements â€“ as exclusion of fixed overheads in valuation of stock. ï‚· Firms might find it difficult to cover all costs and earn a fair return on capital employed AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed analysis of one limitation to a business of using contribution costing L2 1 mark for limited analysis of using contribution costing L1 ï‚· Not acceptable for stock valuation â€“ if stock is undervalued it could lead to government investigation/fines â€“ affect business image ï‚· Unrealistic financial statements â€“ could lead to investors withdrawing from the business or government fines. ï‚· Under-pricing of products â€“ which leads to poor return on capital employed and low profits â€“ shareholder dissatisfaction ï‚· Costs can be difficult to allocate â€“ which leads to inaccurate pricing decisions â€“ lose customer loyalty ï‚· Can lead managers to maintain the production of goods just because of positive contribution â€“ perhaps a brand new product should be launched instead which makes a greater contribution. ï‚· If lower prices are offered to gain customers as contribution can still be earned â€“ may lead to existing customers demanding lower prices â€“ lower profits. ï‚· If high prices are a key feature that has established brand â€“ then lowering prices could destroy brand image. Accept all valid responses. 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 30 Question Answer Marks

5(a) Analyse two benefits to the workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management. There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two benefits to the workforce of McGregorâ€™s Theory X Management: 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management ï‚· Theory X takes the position that employees are reluctant to change and that they require significant external motivation from their managers to complete their work. ï‚· Employees dislike work and avoid it where possible. ï‚· Employees will avoid responsibility and are not creative ï‚· When staff are motivated only by money ï‚· Employees know exactly what is required of them ï‚· Some employees thrive under an authoritative management style ï‚· Employees can focus on achieving goal ï‚· Some employees may not have the skills ï‚· No room for ambiguity ï‚· Benefit new starters or people with lack of experience ï‚· Employees may not want to make decisions in a crisis ï‚· Close supervision can motivate some workers 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 21 of 34 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for application/explanation of one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management ï‚· Can benefit employees who are motivated by money â€“ to achieve targets ï‚· Employees know exactly what is required of them â€“ so can complete work set ï‚· Some employees thrive under an authoritative management style â€“ perhaps if new to a job ï‚· Employees can focus on achieving goal â€“ so they achieve it ï‚· Some employees may not have the skills - to take on more responsibility ï‚· No room for ambiguity â€“ as roles are clear ï‚· Can benefit new starters or people with lack of experience â€“ who need a lot of guidance ï‚· Employees may not want to make decisions in a crisis â€“ and can rely on the manager to do this ï‚· Close supervision can motivate some workers to work more quickly â€“ as they may naturally be lazy AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed analysis of one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management L2 1 mark for limited analysis of one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management L1 ï‚· If employees are motivated by money â€“ theory X may enable targets to be met and subsequent financial rewards. ï‚· Lazy employees are more likely to be kept on track â€“ so less likely to face disciplinary/performance issues ï‚· Removes the stress of having to make decisions (in a crisis) â€“ so better able to cope at work ï‚· Being able to complete work set â€“ means they meet targets set by managers ï‚· Newer employers are able to settle into job faster â€“ might not leave ï‚· Not given additional responsibility â€“ so donâ€™t feel overburdened Accept all valid responses. 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 22 of 34 Q

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Samiraâ€™s Whiteboards (SW) Samira left school at the age of 18 in country H. She had a small amount of savings and an idea to create a flexible, removable and reusable whiteboard. Samira created a prototype and received small orders from local retailers. A local manufacturer batch produces stock when required. There is a two-week lead time for a minimum order of 500 units. Samira started to sell her whiteboards on her website and at trade shows. She has been trading for seven months. She has good cashflow but little working capital as production costs are high. Samira has heard that OS, a large business that sells office equipment, is planning to sell their own version of Samiraâ€™s whiteboard. She is keen to increase production quickly to take advantage of being first to market. However, Samiraâ€™s manufacturer cannot supply enough product to meet the growth in potential demand for whiteboards. Fig. 1.1 shows an inventory control chart for the first seven months of trade. 0 100 200 300 400 500 600 700 800 Jan Feb Mar Apr May Jun July Reorder level Minimum stock level Fig. 1. SW Inventory control chart 2023 Lara, a venture capitalist with experience of manufacturing, has approached Samira about making an investment. She would invest $100 000 to build a local mass production facility for SW. The facility would have a maximum output of 50 000 units per month. Lara wants to own 40% of the business. (a) (i) Identify one traditional function of a manager. [1] (ii) Explain the term working capital . [3] (b) (i) Refer to Fig. 1.1. Calculate the total number of whiteboards sold between January and July. [3] (ii) Explain one cost for SW of holding inventory. [3] (c) Analyse two barriers to entrepreneurship that Samira may have faced when starting up SW. [8] (d) Evaluate whether Samira should accept Lara's offer to invest venture capital. question_text: Post Scent (PS) PS is a private limited company operating in country H. It sells perfume for men and women using its own website. PS purchases the perfume in large quantities from various manufacturers and then sells it in smaller quantities to its customers. Most of the perfumes are sold in glass bottles. PS has two main ways in which it sells perfume: â€¢ A website where customers can purchase a range of perfumes. The perfumes are priced to be lower than high street competitors. â€¢ A subscription service where customers pay a monthly fee for a mystery perfume to be delivered each month. All of PSâ€™s products are delivered to customersâ€™ homes using the postal service. The packages can be left in a mailbox or in a safe place outside of the home. PS has an aim to improve the sustainability of its operations. PS uses incremental budgeting. Table 1.1 shows the budgeted and actual cost and revenue data for 2023. Table 1. question_text: Recruitment Process (RP) RP is a private limited company based in country Z. RP is an employment agency that operates in a dynamic business environment. To survive in the market RP must be efficient and maintain a good reputation. RP uses social media to attract customers and relies on word-of-mouth recommendations. RP has recently won an award for its diversity and equality practices. RPâ€™s customers provide details of the roles and number of employees required. RP quotes a price to its customer and then agrees a contract to provide its services at the price quoted. Ray, Managing Director, has asked Adjil, Finance Director, to provide a budget breakdown of a recent customer contract. RPâ€™s staff are specialist recruiters who find the appropriate employees for each job role. Table 1. Ray is concerned that the budget does not accurately show the costs to RP. Ray thinks this is because of inefficient practices in the recruitment process. (a) (i) Identify one element of a dynamic business environment. [1] (ii) Explain the term employment agency . [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 1.1 and other information. Calculate RPâ€™s actual cost per hire for the customer contract in March 2024. [3] (ii) Explain one benefit to RP of using a budget. INFORMATION â— The total mark for this paper is 60. â— The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. * 0 3 2 4 9 2 5 1 1 0 * DC (SL) 332222/1 2 9609/23/M/J/24 Festival Wear (FW) FW is a partnership started by two friends, Maz and Jane. FW manufactures and sells high quality T-shirts at $40 each. The business can manufacture 35 000 T-shirts per month using mass customisation. The T-shirts are only sold at music festivals and concerts and are customised to each event. FW has never advertised the T-shirts, relying on a sales team to attend a festival or concert and personally sell the T-shirts. Current costs: â€¢ variable: $10 per T-shirt â€¢ fixed: $200 000 per month. Sales average 27 000 T-shirts per month. Currently the sales team is paid a monthly salary, but Maz is proposing a new payment method in order to increase sales volume. This will mean a lower monthly salary but a commission of $2 per T-shirt will be paid. Maz estimates that sales volume would increase to 30 000 per month. Costs if the new payment method is introduced: â€¢ variable: $12 per T-shirt â€¢ fixed: $182 000 per month. This proposal has been discussed with the sales team. Some members of the team are enthusiastic about the proposal, but others are not happy with what they see as a reduction in their pay. The business is expanding and considering moving to a bigger factory. This would require a $4 million investment in new machinery and staff training. Jane would also like to consider methods to increase sales volume. She has suggested that FW should expand its product portfolio to manufacture other items such as baseball caps. (a) (i) Identify one fixed cost. [1] (ii) Explain the term mass customisation . [3] (b) (i) Calculate FWâ€™s break-even output per month if the new payment method is introduced. [3] (ii) Explain one advantage to FWâ€™s employees of the new payment method. [3] (c) Analyse two external sources of finance that FW could use to invest in new machinery. [8] (d) Evaluate whether FW should expand its product portfolio to increase its sales volume. 1(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using empowerment to motivate employees. There are 3 marks for this question: 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one disadvantage of using empowerment to motivate employees â€¢ Employees lack ability/experience to respond â€¢ Decisions are poorer â€¢ Business is less efficient â€¢ Employees become de - motivated â€¢ Employees more stressed â€¢ Some employees may leave. â€¢ Empowerment may not work/may not last 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 12 of 35 Question Answer Marks

1(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the disadvantage given 1 mark for limited of the disadvantage given â€¢ Employees may not be capable of taking more control of their work â€“ might lead to de - motivated employees. â€¢ Empowerment may mean decisions are made by less experienced and less expert personnel â€“ increasing the number of mistakes made and increasing risk. â€¢ May lead to decreased efficiency â€“ autonomous employees may work independently and decide to work more slowly and/or work at the pace of the slowest â€“ less collaboration and teamwork. â€¢ Relationships between managers and employees become blurred â€“ boundaries of authority broken in a flatter, more streamlined structure â€“ managers may lose control. â€¢ Employees stress increases, morale decreases in a preference for alternative forms of motivation. â€¢ Empowered employees become more attractive to competitors (risk of staff poaching). â€¢ May not work as some employees are only motivated by financial benefits e.g. bonus Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 13 of 35 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term price discrimination . [2] (b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using competitive pricing. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term price discrimination. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ The policy of selling the same product at different prices (1) to different buyers (1) Partial understanding â€¢ Selling a product at different prices (1) â€¢ Setting different prices for different people (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 14 of 35 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using competitive pricing. There are 3 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one disadvantage of using competitive pricing â€¢ Competitive pricing may mean a business makes a loss â€¢ Competitive pricing may force prices down/lower in the long turn â€¢ Could lead to business failure â€¢ Could lead to a price war â€¢ Could deflect a business from other objectives â€¢ May mean too much concern for competitor actions. â€¢ May lead to lower/less sales 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 15 of 35 Question Answer Marks

2(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the disadvantage given 1 mark for limited of the disadvantage given â€¢ A business that sets prices solely on competitor prices might risk selling at a loss as it may not be able to cover its manufacturing costs or business overhead expenses. â€¢ Competing using competitor prices (lower) might give a competitive edge in the short term, only as competitors fight back with lower prices. â€¢ Could lead to lower profit margins and increase the risk of failure, especially true of businesses with few financial resources. â€¢ May be difficult and costly to discover competitor prices â€“ need to hire pricing teams or purchasing automated competitor pricing monitoring tool. â€¢ Could lead to a price war and even if a business can sustain low prices, customers may see the low - price product as an inferior product. â€¢ A sole focus on competitive pricing might deflect a business from concerns for quality and added value. â€¢ Too much focus on competitorâ€™s prices may also mean too much time spent on competitors, to the detriment of insufficient time spent on internal issues such as strengthening its USP and competitive edge (other than price points). Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 16 of 35 Question Answer Marks 3 ( a) Define the term capital intensive operations. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ Capital intensive operations involve business processes/production that require large amounts of capital expenditure/machinery/equipment (1) compared to labour

question_text: Define the term capital intensive operations . [2] (b) Explain one limitation to a business of labour intensive operations. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(b) Explain one limitation to a business of labour intensive operations. There are 3 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one limitation of using labour intensive operations â€¢ Expertise, experience of employees can vary â€¢ Skilled workers may take time to train. â€¢ It can be more expensive â€¢ Relatively inefficient and inconsistent/unreliable levels of effort/not as fast as machines â€¢ Labour relations problems. â€¢ Difficulty of replacing/recruiting staff. 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 18 of 35 Question Answer Marks

3(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the limitation given 1 mark for limited of the limitation given Production processes that have a high level of labour input and a low level of capital input may have the following limitations: â€¢ Variable levels of labour expertise and experience may lead to variable levels of product quality. â€¢ The training and re - training of employees may be very expensive and may disrupt the production process. â€¢ Due to lower productivity the unit cost may be higher than capital intensive operations â€¢ Unable to reach the consistency levels of machine production - employees fall ill and take holidays. â€¢ Employees may seek better conditions leading to disputes, strikes, unionisation. â€¢ Labour turnover may be high, competitive markets for labour may mean labour shortages - growth targets threatened. Accept all valid responses 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 19 of 35 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one way a business might fail as a consequence of lack of finance. There are 5 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one way a business might fail due to lack of finance Inability to: â€¢ sustain cashflows â€¢ provide working capital â€¢ build up reserves â€¢ pay suppliers/variable costs â€¢ invest â€¢ obtain sources of finance â€¢ maintain quality/innovation/research and development â€¢ to recruit managers â€¢ bankruptcy/insolvency/administration/liquidity issues 5 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 20 of 35 Question Answer Marks 4 AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the chosen way identified 1 mark for limited of the chosen way given â€¢ A lack of finance may undermine a businessâ€™s capacity to survive at various times and in various situation â€¢ Such as the inability to purchase essential equipment â€¢ An inability to cover day - to - day expenses (paying wages and suppliers) â€¢ An inability to build up reserves in times of recession â€¢ An inability to support essential growth and expansion plans.

Stimulates and improves social welfare â€“ such as electricity for all AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed of one chosen way â€“ repeat for second identified way 1 mark for limited of one chosen way â€“ repeat for second identified way â€¢ Business enterprise stimulates new business formation, providing choice, dynamism, competition and employment, often locally owned and committed to local areas, and act as seedbeds for further development. â€¢ Business enterprise cultures lead to the creation of new jobs and opportunities â€“ often entry level jobs to turn unskilled job holders into skilled ones and prepares and provides experienced works for larger industries. â€¢ Business enterprise initiatives bring innovation and creativity to countries â€“ resources are used effectively as new and improved products, services, and technologies are developed, leading to the generation of new wealth, higher tax revenues, allowing gov ernments to offer citizens more benefits. â€¢ Business enterprise encourages research and development to solve problems that existing products and technology have not yet solved â€“ bring innovation to existing products â€“ secure new finance and experiment with new approaches to business functions, such as HRM, Operations, Finance, and Marketing. â€¢ The economic benefit of business enterprise may create the income and wealth for social change and development by reducing a dependency on

question_text: Natural Shampoos (NS) NS is a private limited company set up by Saira and Chantal as a social enterprise. NS sells natural hair care products and focuses on customer (market) orientation. The main aims of Saira and Chantal include donating to local charities and improving gender equality in the workplace. NS began by selling its products to small, independent businesses in country H. NS expanded its range slowly based on customer feedback, specific requests on its blog, and sales data. NS explored additional finance opportunities required for expansion. NS approached John, who owns manufacturing facilities in country H and has significant marketing experience. He invested into NS in return for ownership of one third of the business. Johnâ€™s main aim is to increase long-term profits in order to maximise the return on his investment. In the five years since Johnâ€™s investment, NS has become the market leader in country H for natural hair care products. NS products are now sold in major cosmetic retailers. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Crafty Toys (CT) CT is a co-operative that produces and sells a range of wooden toys, such as puzzles, cars and dolls. The co-operative was formed in 2020 by a group of 12 people who share a passion for making toys. They wanted to work together to create quality products that would appeal to children and parents. No one person takes on the role of manager; instead, they share the traditional manager functions between them. CT operates from a large workshop in a rural area of country Z, making toys using labour-intensive techniques. CT uses locally sourced wood that is certified as eco-friendly. CT also uses natural paints and finishes that are safe and non-toxic for children. Table 1.1 shows CTâ€™s output in 2024. Table 1. Output data for CT in 2024 Number of people Average hours worked per person per year Output 12 1800 54 000 units CT sells its products online through its own website and social media platforms, as well as through some independent retailers who specialise in ethical products. The co-operative also attends various trade fairs and exhibitions to showcase its products and network with potential customers and suppliers. CT has never researched customer wants. CT faces some challenges in its current operations. These challenges include: â€¢ high costs of production â€¢ a long lead time â€¢ a requirement for skilled workers. CT is considering changing to flow production to overcome these challenges. 5 10 15 Extract from FFâ€™s financial data for 2024 $000 Revenue 120 Direct costs
